data.title: TotalEnergies CEO rejects accusations of crimes at Mozambique project
data.description:
TotalEnergies (TTE) CEO Patrick Pouyanne pushed back against accusations that the company has responsibilities in the alleged killing of civilians four years ago at its Mozambique LNG project site, Bloomberg reported Wednesday.

The European Center for Constitutional and Human Rights filed a criminal complaint this week with the French National Anti-Terrorism Prosecutor, accusing TotalEnergies (TTE) of "complicity in war crimes" through a financial link to a Mozambican army unit that allegedly held civilians in shipping containers where dozens of them were tortured and killed at the company's project between July and September 2021. 

"We will defend ourselves and we will explain that all this has nothing to do with TotalEnergies," Pouyanne said Wednesday. "We've done inquiries [and] we never managed to find evidence" of the allegations.

The complaint comes as TotalEnergies (TTE) is preparing to restart construction of the project for the first time since the site was shut in 2021 due to an Islamist insurgency.

"Our reputation is under attack once again because we're developing one of the largest gas fields in the world in Mozambique," the CEO said.

data.title: Lowe's outlines $86B sales target for 2025 while advancing Pro strategy through FBM acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Marvin Ellison, President, CEO & Chairman, reported "third quarter sales were $20.8 billion with comparable sales increasing 0.4% year-over-year, despite a roughly 100 basis point headwind related to Hurricanes Helene and Milton." He highlighted adjusted operating margin expansion and an adjusted diluted earnings per share of $3.06, up 6% versus last year. Ellison emphasized continued progress with the Total Home Strategy, notably with "growth this quarter" in the small to medium Pro segment, online sales growth of 11.4%, and double-digit comps in home services.
  • The CEO detailed the strategic importance of generative AI, noting virtual assistants answer nearly 1 million customer questions per month, and stated that "when our customers engage with Mylow online, the conversion rate more than doubles." AI adoption earned Lowe’s the OpenAI 100 billion token milestone award.
  • Ellison announced the completed acquisition of Foundation Building Materials (FBM) in October, describing it as giving Lowe’s "a more comprehensive product portfolio, expands our revenue streams and further enhances our offering to our Pro customers." He stated that FBM’s 370 locations will strengthen fulfillment, especially in high-density urban markets, and efforts are underway to quickly connect FBM’s product catalog to Lowe’s Pro extended aisle.
  • William Boltz, Executive Vice President of Merchandising, noted "positive comps in 10 of our 14 merchandise divisions" and highlighted performance in appliances, flooring, paint, kitchens and bath, and building products. Boltz announced the launch of Sherwin-Williams ProBlock Quick Dry primers, exclusive to Lowe’s and Sherwin-Williams, and described the rollout of the rural format to nearly 500 stores and the introduction of the Heart & Herd private pet brand.
  • Joseph McFarland, Executive Vice President of Stores, stated that new training programs and AI-powered tools are "boosting their knowledge, confidence and effectiveness at driving sales." He also reported double-digit growth in Home Services and ongoing improvements in inventory shrink.
  • Brandon Sink, Executive VP & CFO, said, "we generated GAAP diluted earnings per share of $2.88." Sink reported $105 million in pretax transaction costs for the FBM acquisition and $24 million in non-GAAP adjustments for ADG. He highlighted a comparable average ticket increase of 3.4% and a comparable transaction decline of 3%. Sink concluded, "excluding these impacts, we delivered adjusted diluted earnings per share of $3.06, exceeding our expectations."

Outlook

  • The company updated its outlook to expect "comp sales to be roughly flat for the year, which is at the bottom end of our previous guidance." Including FBM, Lowe’s expects full-year sales of approximately $86 billion and an adjusted operating margin of approximately 12.1%, including 20 basis points of dilution from FBM and ADG. Adjusted diluted earnings per share are expected to be approximately $12.25. Capital expenditures are projected at up to $2.5 billion for the year.
  • FBM and ADG are expected to negatively impact the consolidated adjusted operating margin by approximately 50 basis points on an annualized basis.

Financial Results

  • Third quarter sales were $20.8 billion, with comparable sales up 0.4%. Gross margin was 34.2% in the quarter, up 50 basis points. Adjusted SG&A was 19.6% of sales, and adjusted operating margin rate was 12.4%, up 10 basis points versus prior year. Inventory ended Q3 at $17.2 billion, down approximately $400 million versus prior year, including $600 million from recent acquisitions. Operating cash flow for Q3 was $687 million; capital expenditures were $597 million; dividends paid were $673 million. Return on invested capital was 26.1%.
  • The company financed the FBM transaction using $5 billion of bonds, a $2 billion term loan, and $1.8 billion in cash.

Q&A

  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: Asked about business trends and guidance for Q4 and 2026. Marvin Ellison responded that "we're very pleased with the positive comp performance to start the quarter in spite of storm overlaps from last year" and sees "acceleration on 1-year comps when you exclude storm-related activity." William Boltz highlighted broad-based early strength in Q4, especially in seasonal categories and appliances.
  • Horvers asked about kitchen and bath trends. Ellison said, "this is more about lowest taking share in this space," citing technology improvements and central selling. Boltz noted "broad-based strength, toilets, bathing, faucets, disposable kitchen sinks, bath repair."
  • Zachary Fadem, Wells Fargo: Asked about improving Pro sentiment. Ellison reported "roughly 75% of our Pro's are very confident in their job prospects." McFarland discussed the "flywheel effect" from Pro loyalty programs and digital investments. Sink addressed FBM and ADG margin impact.
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about macro scenario and housing affordability. Ellison emphasized the "lock-in effect" and strong homeowner equity, noting "we think HELOCs are going to become the next opportunity for us to drive discretionary remodel big-ticket projects." Sink added that "HELOC rates go from neighborhood of 10% to 12% down to 8% to 10%."
  • Katharine McShane, Goldman Sachs: Asked about marketplace performance. Ellison stated, "it's really early... exceeding expectations relative to financial performance, exceeding expectations, relative to the number of sellers and the quality of sellers."
  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: Asked about operating margin sustainability. Sink said "very focused at this point on delivering that the 12.1% operating margin that we communicated as part of our guide."
  • Gregory Melich, Evercore ISI: Inquired about traffic and ticket breakdown. Sink explained "strength in our Pro business, also appliances," and "modest price increases."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on strategic execution and integration risks, while raising questions about margins, consumer trends, and the impact of acquisitions.
  • Management presented a confident and optimistic sentiment in prepared remarks, with Ellison stating, "we remain confident that the continued execution of our Total Home Strategy will position Lowe's to win in the short and in the long term." During Q&A, management remained constructive but cautious on macro uncertainties, especially regarding housing and consumer demand.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains confident but with increased attention to integration and macro headwinds. Analysts in both quarters focused on margin sustainability and strategic direction, but Q3 featured more questions about acquisition impacts and marketplace scaling.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a range to the "bottom end" for comp sales, now expected to be flat for the year, versus prior quarter’s flat to up 1% range.
  • The previous quarter emphasized the upcoming FBM acquisition; this quarter discussed the completed integration and its financial impacts.
  • FBM and ADG margin dilution was quantified more explicitly this quarter, with an annualized 50 basis points impact articulated.
  • Management’s confidence in AI, Pro strategy, and marketplace expansion has grown, with new performance data shared.
  • Analysts continued to focus on Pro trends, macro risks, and integration, but questions shifted toward operational execution and platform scalability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macroeconomic uncertainty, consumer confidence, and high borrowing costs were cited as ongoing challenges.
  • Integration of FBM and ADG poses operational and margin risks; management is "already working collaboratively... on cross-selling opportunities" and "cost synergies."
  • Tariff impacts are expected to continue ramping into Q4 and the first half of next year.
  • Management highlighted cautious consumer behavior and the potential delay in housing recovery.

Final Takeaway

Lowe’s management highlighted progress in its Total Home Strategy, growth in Pro and online segments, and the strategic integration of FBM and ADG, which are expected to expand product offerings and enhance fulfillment capabilities. The company updated its sales target to $86 billion for the year, with a flat comp sales outlook and projected adjusted diluted EPS of $12.25. While macroeconomic and integration risks remain, management expressed confidence in long-term growth, supported by AI adoption, operational discipline, and continued investment in customer experience and productivity initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cerence signals 23% revenue growth target for 2026 amid aggressive IP monetization and xUI expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cerence Inc. (CRNC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Krzanich highlighted, "Over the past year, we've strengthened the financial and operational foundation of the company and significantly increased positive cash flow generation, beating nearly every metric and putting us on a solid ground for executing on our future growth plans." Krzanich emphasized the successful resolution of a suit with Samsung, resulting in a $49.5 million lump sum payment, calling it "an important milestone in our strategy and a proof point for the broad applicability of our technology across different industries and verticals."
  • Krzanich detailed progress with the xUI platform, stating, "We've made significant progress with our xUI platform, including meeting all our technology milestones while driving strong customer interest and adoption."
  • The company reported new deals with Toyota, Ford, BMW, Honda, Great Wall Motor, and an autonomous trucking company, and noted nine programs started production in Q4, including BYD, Subaru, and Geely.
  • The CEO shared that cost management remains a priority, with a new restructuring plan for certain foreign operations initiated in Q4, aiming to "further reduce operating expenses and position Cerence for profitable, sustainable future growth."
  • CFO Antonio Rodriquez stated, "Q4 total revenue was $60.6 million, surpassing our projected range... Variable license revenue for the quarter was $31.6 million, up 25% year-over-year... Q4 connected service revenue came in at $14.2 million, up 17% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Cerence expects fiscal year 2026 revenue in the range of $300 million to $320 million, marking a 23% year-over-year increase at the midpoint, including the $49.5 million Samsung patent license payment. The company anticipates 8% growth in the core technology business and expects adjusted EBITDA between $50 million and $70 million, with free cash flow of $56 million to $66 million. Professional services revenue is projected to shrink as new technologies streamline implementations.
  • For Q1 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected between $110 million and $120 million, incorporating the Samsung payment, and adjusted EBITDA is projected between $30 million and $40 million. Gross margins for the full year are expected to be between 79% and 80%.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue reached $60.6 million, above the guidance range, while adjusted EBITDA was $8.3 million. Full fiscal year revenue totaled $251.8 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $48.1 million. Free cash flow for the year grew to $46.8 million.
  • Variable license revenue was $31.6 million, reflecting a shift toward usage-based models. Connected services revenue for the year was $53.4 million, with a 14% year-over-year increase after adjusting for a prior noncash benefit.
  • Gross profit for Q4 was $44 million, with a 73% gross margin. Non-GAAP operating expenses were $38.3 million, down 3% year-over-year, and the GAAP net loss for Q4 narrowed to $13.4 million.
  • Total debt was reduced by $87.5 million, ending the year with $87 million in cash and marketable securities.
  • Approximately 11.7 million units were shipped in Q4, and the PPU metric rose to $5.05 for the trailing 12 months, up 12% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Jeff Van Rhee, Craig-Hallum: Asked about the profit flow-through from the Samsung IP settlement and legal expense assumptions for 2026. Antonio Rodriquez explained, "We expect that to flow through revenue so that -- at a gross amount of $49.5 million... the net amount would be minus roughly 24, call it, $24 million of legal cost and a bit of withholding tax as well."
  • Van Rhee asked about POC ramp for xUI. Brian Krzanich replied, "We have about half a dozen POCs going on with different OEMs in various levels of the xUI platform."
  • On connected revenue, Van Rhee questioned whether there were any accounting pull-forwards. Krzanich stated, "There's really only one way I know of... it's always over the life of the contract. So there's no pull forwards or unique accounting."
  • Will Bryant (for Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs): Queried the 8% core business growth for 2026. Rodriquez described, "That's roughly 8% or 9% in the connected side. And similarly, a similar percentage in license... more of it will be in-period revenue for those shipments."
  • Bryant also sought an update on the competitive landscape. Krzanich indicated, "The competitive landscape hasn't necessarily changed dramatically... more and more of the technology is becoming large language model based... Google is there, Amazon is there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive, pressing for detail on IP monetization, legal expenses, growth drivers, and competitive positioning, but not overtly negative or confrontational.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases such as "we are confident" and "we believe that Cerence has the right foundation for long-term sustainable growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both analysts and management remains stable, with management showing increased confidence due to the successful Samsung settlement and improved financials.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a major strategic milestone with the Samsung IP settlement, and guidance for 2026 now incorporates a significant one-time revenue event and a shift to recurring IP monetization.
  • The company's 2026 revenue growth target is more ambitious than previous guidance, reflecting higher confidence in technology adoption and IP strategies. Core business growth expectations are now more clearly broken out.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on growth drivers, competitive landscape, and legal/IP monetization, but this quarter had more focus on the explicit financial impact of the Samsung deal and legal cost structure.
  • Management's tone grew more assertive, with explicit statements of confidence in the company’s direction and financial health.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that the IP monetization process involves long litigation timelines and potential cost risks, with legal expenses incorporated into guidance.
  • The company continues to face competitive pressure from large tech players, with the CEO noting, "more and more of the technology is becoming large language model based."
  • Professional services revenue is expected to decline as OEMs build internal capabilities, potentially impacting revenue mix.
  • While debt reduction has strengthened the balance sheet, ongoing legal costs and restructuring expenses remain relevant factors.

Final Takeaway

Cerence closed fiscal 2025 with strong cash generation, improved profitability, and a foundational win in IP monetization through the Samsung settlement. The company enters 2026 targeting robust revenue growth, propelled by expansion of the xUI platform, new customer wins, and a multi-year IP monetization roadmap, while keeping a sharp focus on disciplined cost management and operational efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dominion in talks to buy Virginia's NOVEC in data center bet - Bloomberg
data.description:
Dominion Energy (D) is in talks to buy the Northern Virginia Electric Cooperative, in a move that would expand the utility's reach in a key part of the global data center market, Bloomberg reported Wednesday.

A purchase of NOVEC would provide opportunities for Dominion (D) to serve the buildout of data centers in Northern Virginia, where NOVEC has multiple data center customers, according to the report.

NOVEC provides power to more than 180,000 homes and businesses in Northern Virginia, and served ~60 data centers, generating ~$1 billion in revenue last year; data centers account for about two-thirds of its electricity sales, which is expected to expand rapidly with more projects being planned.

Dominion (D) and NOVEC's systems already are interlaced, with Dominion's transmission services tied to NOVEC's substations that are dedicated to data centers.

data.title: Oil futures fall as U.S. pushes new plan to end Russia-Ukraine war
data.description:
Crude oil futures fell Wednesday as U.S. efforts to restart peace talks between Russia and Ukraine took some risk premium out of the market, although steep early losses were contained following data from the Energy Information Administration showing an unexpected draw in U.S. crude inventories.

The framework of the peace plan plan would require Ukraine to give up some land and reduce the size of its armed forces, according to published reports; Ukraine apparently had no role in preparing the proposals.

"There is maximum pressure right now as Friday's deadline is looming" for the start of sanctions against Russian oil producers Rosneft and Lukoil, Rystad Energy oil analyst Janiv Shah said in a note, adding that a lower geopolitical risk premium would leave investors focusing more closely on weak market fundamentals.

The EIA reported U.S. crude inventories fell by 3.4 million barrels during the week ended November 14, leaving them about 5% below the seasonal five year average, along with a 2.3 million barrel build in gasoline inventories and a 200,000 barrel increase in distillate supplies during the week; despite the increases, gasoline remains 3% below seasonal averages while distillate supplies are 7% lower.

The EIA also reported U.S. implied gasoline demand, as measured by products supplied, fell by 500,000 bbl/day to 8.528 million bbl/day; the decline was expected, as the first week of November typically sees a high demand reading.

Both major crude oil benchmarks closed -2.1%, with front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for December delivery and front-month Brent January crude (CO1:COM) settling at $59.44/bbl and $63.51/bbl, respectively; distillate futures, which have been supporting crude, were lower with Nymex diesel off 2.1%.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) rose as weather forecasts turned colder for the last few days of November, with the front-month Nymex December contract closing +4.1% to $4.550/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: American Strategic Investment signals $99M debt elimination through property disposition as portfolio transitions continue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Strategic Investment Co. (NYC) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Nicholas Schorsch indicated that the quarter's focus was on proactive management, cost control, and efforts to unlock value at current assets. He highlighted a major lease renewal at 196 Orchard, which increased the weighted average remaining lease term to 6.2 years and noted, "56% of our leases now extend beyond 2030, up from 54% last quarter."
  • Schorsch described a strategic move to dispose of 1140 Avenue of the Americas, stating, "Upon completion, this transaction is expected to eliminate a $99 million liability that matures in July 2026."
  • The company continues to market 123 William Street and 196 Orchard for sale, with the intent to use proceeds to reduce debt and reinvest in higher-yielding assets.
  • Michael LeSanto, CFO, stated, "Third quarter 2025 revenue was $12.3 million compared to $15.4 million in the third quarter of 2024, principally due to the sale of 9 Times Square in the fourth quarter of 2024. The company's GAAP net gain attributable to common stockholders was $35.8 million in the third quarter of 2025, impacted by a $44.3 million noncash gain related to the foreclosure at 1140 Avenue of the Americas."
  • LeSanto added, "We also proactively and significantly reduced our professional fees by looking to change our audit partners via the engagement of CBIZ CPAs as our new independent registered public accounting firm for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2025."

Outlook

  • Schorsch emphasized ongoing initiatives to enhance operational flexibility through property dispositions and reinvestment: "We believe these sales will generate cash on the balance sheet that can be deployed into higher-yielding assets, creating future value for the portfolio."
  • The company is focused on lease renewals, tenant retention, and cost controls as it prepares to present full-year 2025 results, but no explicit new guidance numbers were provided in the current transcript.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q3 2025 was $12.3 million, with a GAAP net gain attributable to common stockholders of $35.8 million, reflecting a $44.3 million noncash gain from the 1140 Avenue of the Americas foreclosure.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $1.9 million, and cash net operating income was $5.3 million.
  • The company proactively reduced professional fees through a change in audit partners.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE. The transcript did not contain a Q&A session or analyst questions to management.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment cannot be assessed as no analyst questions or commentary were present in the transcript.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was methodical and focused on operational discipline, with an emphasis on cost controls, strategic asset sales, and portfolio stability. Schorsch's remarks, including "We remain committed to operating and unlocking value at our current assets," reflected a focus on resilience and proactive management.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similar pragmatic tone but placed greater emphasis on balance sheet management and expense reduction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced the strategic consensual foreclosure of 1140 Avenue of the Americas, aiming to eliminate a $99 million liability, whereas the prior quarter noted the initiation of foreclosure proceedings but not the resolution.
  • The weighted average remaining lease term increased from 6.0 to 6.2 years, with a higher portion of leases now extending beyond 2030 (56% up from 54%).
  • The company continued to market properties for sale, with no major shift in strategic focus but heightened attention to debt reduction and reinvestment in higher-yielding assets.
  • Management's language became more focused on cost cutting, professional fee reduction, and the transition to new auditors, compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on stability and ongoing sale processes.
  • Key financial metrics such as revenue and cash net operating income were lower than the same period last year but were up from the previous quarter ($12.3 million revenue vs. $12.2 million in Q2 2025; $5.3 million cash NOI vs. $4.2 million in Q2 2025).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the challenge of ongoing and upfront expenses required to operate and lease 1140 Avenue of the Americas as a reason for the consensual foreclosure.
  • The company continues to face potential risks related to property sales and the ability to close on favorable terms for 123 William Street and 196 Orchard.
  • Schorsch described efforts to mitigate risks by focusing on tenant retention, cost efficiency, and strategic asset sales to strengthen the balance sheet.

Final Takeaway

American Strategic Investment Co. placed significant emphasis this quarter on cost control, strengthening its balance sheet, and repositioning its portfolio through key property dispositions. The anticipated elimination of a $99 million liability through the consensual foreclosure of 1140 Avenue of the Americas and ongoing efforts to sell additional properties represent critical steps in the company’s strategy to redeploy capital into assets with higher return potential, while maintaining an operational focus on tenant retention and expense management as year-end approaches.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FirstEnergy slapped with $250M in fines, refunds by Ohio regulators in bribery scandal
data.description:
FirstEnergy (FE) was ordered by the Public Utilities Commission of Ohio to pay more than $250 million in fines and refunds, after finding the utility company violated multiple rules and misused ratepayer money in the state's sweeping HB6 bribery scandal five years ago, the Associated Press reported Wednesday.

The orders concluded three separate investigations of FirstEnergy (FE) that had been delayed by an ongoing Justice Department probe that burst into public view in July 2020 when Ohio House Speaker Larry Householder and four associates were arrested and charged in an alleged $60 million racketeering scheme funded by the company in exchange for a $1 billion nuclear plant bailout.

FirstEnergy (FE) later admitted to the bribes and agreed to pay $230 million to avoid prosecution, fired CEO Chuck Jones and other executives accused of involvement in the bribes, and reformed its ethics policies and code of conduct, but PUCO said Wednesday that the company must still face regulatory consequences for its actions.

data.title: FDA testing program to boost communication with drugmakers during reviews
data.description:
• Since October, the US FDA has been running a trial of a program that aims to have more frequent communication with pharma companies that have drug applications pending with the agency to ease the process.
  • FDA Commissioner Marty Makary said that the trial has only been with a handful of companies, but has been going well. One new element is that if a drugmaker has a question, they can send it to FDA with a turnaround time within three business days.
  • "If they can get that clarification, it can save them months of guesswork," Makary told Bloomberg in an interview. 
  • While the new program is being spearheaded by the Office of New Drugs, the commissioner is aiming to expand it to its other divisions.
  • Makary added the agency intends to hire 1,000 new scientists and inspectors over the next few months.
  • Earlier this year, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced thousands of layoffs at the FDA.

data.title: NOV CEO Williams set to retire, succeeded by Bayardo
data.description:
NOV Inc. (NOV) said Wednesday that Chairman and CEO Clay Williams will retire, to be succeeded by current President and COO Jose Bayardo, who will become Chairman, President and CEO effective January 1.

Bayardo joined NOV (NOV) in 2015 as Senior VP and CFO before being promoted to President and COO earlier this year; before joining the company, he held executive roles at Continental Resources and Complete Production Services.

Williams became NOV's (NOV) Chairman and CEO in 2014 after holding a variety of other roles at the company, including CFO during 2005-12 and COO in 2012-14; before the merger of National Oilwell and Varco in 2005, he worked for Tuboscope, SCF Partners and Shell Oil.

data.title: Roger Mobley promoted to CFO of commercial bank
data.description:
• Commercial Bancgroup (CBK) on Wednesday announced the promotion of Roger Mobley to Chief Financial Officer of Commercial Bank effective November 1, 2025, succeeding Philip Metheny.
  • Philip Metheny will remain the CFO of Commercial Bancgroup (CBI).
  • Mobley joined Commercial Bank on July 1, 2024 with the acquisition of Alliance Bank & Trust Company (AB&T). Since that time, he has served as Assistant CFO for Commercial Bank and has been instrumental in the transition of the CBI from private to a public company.
  • Source: Press Release
